L1.area: Authentication middleware and token caching infrastructure

L2.description: A cryptographic weakness exists in the middleware that protects cached authentication data. The implementation uses a weak hashing algorithm for computing message authentication codes, derives encryption keys via simple hash concatenation rather than a standards-compliant key derivation function, uses an unauthenticated cipher mode, and does not use constant-time comparison when verifying signatures. These weaknesses allow attackers to bypass cache data integrity and confidentiality protections through timing attacks and cryptographic analysis.

L3.area: Memcache cryptographic protection layer within the authentication middleware
L3.description: A cryptographic weakness exists in the middleware that protects cached authentication tokens stored in memcache. The implementation uses a weak hashing algorithm for computing message authentication codes, derives encryption keys via simple hash concatenation rather than a standards-compliant key derivation function, uses an unauthenticated cipher mode, and does not use constant-time comparison when verifying signatures. This allows attackers to bypass the integrity and confidentiality protections on cached token data through timing attacks and cryptographic analysis.

ground_truth.reason: The memcache encryption middleware uses weak cryptographic primitives including SHA1 for MAC computation, simple hash concatenation for key derivation instead of a proper KDF, an unauthenticated cipher mode (AES-CFB), and non-constant-time comparison for signature verification, allowing attackers to bypass cache data protection.
